---
title: "Palmer Penguins — our real-data running example"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
---

This page describes the **Palmer Penguins** dataset, which we use **all week** for plots, regression (predicting a number), and classification (predicting a category). **Tuesday’s slides** walk through a full **prediction workflow in R** using the same penguin table (the code uses the `tidymodels` family of packages — you do not need to have seen them before Monday’s `lm` / `glm` work).

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
## Synthetic gene notebooks (unchanged)

For teaching **LASSO, ridge, and AIC**, we keep **simulated gene** notebooks where **we wrote the rules ourselves**, so we know which coefficients *should* matter — they are not replaced by penguins:

- [linear-regression-lasso.Rmd](notebooks/linear-regression-lasso.Rmd)  
- [logistic-regression-gene-disease.Rmd](notebooks/logistic-regression-gene-disease.Rmd)  

Palmer Penguins examples live in **additional** notebooks (linked below).
:::

## What it is

The **Palmer Archipelago** penguins dataset (Horst *et al.*, 2020) bundles size measurements for three penguin species. In R you can load it after installing the **`palmerpenguins`** add-on package.

```r
library(palmerpenguins)
data(penguins, package = "palmerpenguins")
```

## Variables (344 rows)

| Variable | Meaning |
|----------|---------|
| `species` | Adelie, Chinstrap, or Gentoo |
| `island` | Torgersen, Biscoe, or Dream |
| `bill_length_mm`, `bill_depth_mm` | Bill size (mm) |
| `flipper_length_mm` | Flipper length (mm) |
| `body_mass_g` | Body mass (g) |
| `sex` | female / male (some `NA`) |
| `year` | Study year (2007, 2008, 2009) |

## Why we use it in this course

- **Plots read well in class** (colour by `species`, pairs of measurements).
- **Same table everywhere** — Monday exploratory plots, Tuesday’s full **fit–check–tune** example in R, Thursday’s discussion of scores and explanations.
- You can practice **regression** (for example predicting **body mass**), **two-group classification** (one species pair, or **sex**), and **three-species** classification on one familiar dataset.

## Student notebooks (Palmer Penguins only)

- [penguins-body-mass.Rmd](notebooks/penguins-body-mass.Rmd) — explore the data, then predict **body mass** from bill and flipper measurements (with shrinkage methods you can compare to `lm`).  
- [penguins-classification.Rmd](notebooks/penguins-classification.Rmd) — **Adelie vs Gentoo**: logistic regression and a classification tree, confusion table, simple 2D “who would we call Gentoo?” picture.  
- [penguins-sex-classification.Rmd](notebooks/penguins-sex-classification.Rmd) — predict **female vs male** from size measures ( **`species` is left out on purpose** so the task is not trivial — discuss why in class).  
- [penguins-species-multiclass.Rmd](notebooks/penguins-species-multiclass.Rmd) — **all three species**: multinomial logistic model and a tree, test-set confusion tables.

## Caveats (say these out loud)

- **Small sample** (~344 rows after dropping missing values) — use for pedagogy, not for “big data” claims.
- **Observational** — strong predictions do **not** prove causal mechanisms in biology.
- **Missing sex** — many analyses simply **drop rows with incomplete sex**; in more advanced work you would **fill in or model missingness** inside the same workflow you use to fit the predictor (so you do not sneak information from the test fold into the training fold).
- **Leakage awareness** — if the prediction task is trivial given the inputs (e.g. near-perfect separation), use it to discuss **evaluation** and **honest splits**, not to oversell accuracy.

## Citation

Horst AM, Hill AP, Gorman KB (2020). *palmerpenguins: Palmer Archipelago (Antarctica) penguin data.* R package version 0.1.1.  
DOI: [10.5281/zenodo.3960218](https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.3960218).
